sections.title: The Big Picture
paragraphs: The 12-team College Football Playoff sets the structure: four autobids for SEC, Big Ten, ACC, and Big 12 champions, with the remaining eight slots filled by résumé and rating. We simulated the full 2026 season 1,000 times — every game, every conference championship, every bracket — using a blended preseason rating model. No two runs produced the same playoff field.

That variance is the headline. Across 1,000 unique 12-team fields, Power Four leagues average 10.3 teams per bracket — the SEC (~2.9), Big Ten (~3.5), Big 12 (~2.0), and ACC (~2.0) supply most of the inventory. Notre Dame, without a conference autobid, still appears in roughly 72% of fields. A Group of 6 program shows up in about 94% of simulations.

National title equity is concentrated in the Big Ten: the league accounts for roughly 42% of all championship odds, led by Ohio State. The SEC follows at about 28%. The model does not reward reputation on its own — it rewards rating, schedule, and the paths that survive a thousand different Novembers.

sections.title: The National Championship Race
paragraphs: Ohio State leads the title race at about 12% — the highest single-program share in the model, though far from a majority. Notre Dame (~9%), Indiana (~9%), and Oregon (~9%) form a crowded tier behind the Buckeyes. Georgia (~6%) is the SEC's top championship equity; Texas Tech (~5%) leads the Big 12.

The gap between making the playoff and winning it matters. Notre Dame reaches the field far more often than it hoists the trophy; Indiana and Oregon pair strong title odds with top-tier playoff frequency. Texas Tech averages nearly 10 wins and carries more national equity than traditional Big 12 powers in this rating snapshot.

Below the top tier, Miami (~5%), Texas (~4%), Alabama (~3%), and Penn State (~3%) remain live long shots. No program clears 13% title equity — this is a fragmented race where seeding, matchup luck, and a single upset can swing January outcomes.

featured_teams.caption: Big Ten favorite · 9.31 avg wins · 27.6% conf title

sections.title: The Playoff Landscape
paragraphs: Playoff frequency separates the locks from the hopefuls. Notre Dame clears the bar in 71.5% of simulations — the model's most reliable field presence. Indiana (60.9%), Oregon (55.5%), Texas Tech (55.2%), and Ohio State (55.1%) form the next tier. Miami (50.6%) and Georgia (44.0%) round out the programs that make the bracket in nearly half of all runs or better.

The most common co-occurrences in playoff fields run through South Bend. Indiana and Notre Dame share a bracket in 42.3% of sims; Oregon–Notre Dame appears in 40.0%; Texas Tech–Notre Dame in 39.8%; Ohio State–Notre Dame in 38.8%. The Irish are the gravitational center — contenders are often judged by whether they share a field with Notre Dame.

The bubble lives in the low twenties: Texas (28.6%), Alabama (28.2%), Texas A&M (26.9%), LSU (23.1%), Louisville (22.8%), and SMU (22.3%) are one hot streak from a bid and one bad Saturday from the bowl slate. With 1,000 distinct fields, there is no single 'last team in' — only probabilities.

sections.title: What to Watch
paragraphs: Schedule strength drives variance. Texas faces the toughest opponent slate in the SEC (SOS +10.4 in the model); Ohio State (+6.8) and Michigan (+7.6) navigate a demanding Big Ten. Teams with soft schedules can pile up wins but still need rating to survive committee-style selection in the sims.

The Group of 6 autobid path is nearly automatic: at least one G6 team appears in 93.8% of playoff fields. James Madison (7.1% field rate, 8.86 avg wins) is the Sun Belt's strongest bid candidate. Boise State (8.4%) leads the Pac-12 footprint. Toledo (4.3%) and North Dakota State (5.5%) are the MAC and Mountain West programs most likely to represent their leagues.

Watch the independents and the coin-flip games. Notre Dame's schedule is light on paper (SOS +0.2) but the Irish still reach the field more than anyone. Late-season conference matchups with win probabilities near 50% — especially in the SEC and Big Ten — are where playoff pictures fracture across the 1,000 runs.

The SEC supplies 28.1% of national title equity and averages 2.9 teams per playoff field — the deepest inventory of any league. Georgia leads the conference race at 17.2% title odds with 44.0% playoff frequency. Texas (13.0% conf title, 28.6% playoff) and Alabama (11.4%, 28.2%) chase the Bulldogs despite brutal schedules.

Texas carries the league's highest SOS (+10.4) — a gauntlet that caps win totals but keeps playoff equity alive. Oklahoma (17.9% playoff) is still climbing in year three of SEC membership. The conference autobid is rarely in doubt; the fight is for seeds, byes, and survival in November.

No league concentrates championship equity like the Big Ten: 41.7% of all national title odds across Ohio State (12.4%), Indiana (9.25%), Oregon (9.25%), and Penn State (2.74%). The conference places a member in every simulated playoff field and averages 3.5 teams per bracket.

Indiana is the breakout — 60.9% playoff frequency and 9.68 avg wins despite a light SOS (+1.7). Ohio State's path is harder (+6.8 SOS) but the Buckeyes remain the league favorite at 27.6% conference title odds. Purdue (3.9 avg wins) anchors the bottom; the middle is crowded with playoff fringe teams like Iowa and Michigan.

Miami dominates the ACC model: 34.7% conference title odds, 50.6% playoff frequency, and 4.97% national title equity — nearly half the league's championship share. The Hurricanes average 9.45 wins and win the ACC title game in nearly two-thirds of appearances.

Louisville (22.8% playoff), SMU (22.3%), and Clemson (17.9%) form a second tier with paths to the field but minimal title equity. The ACC averages 2.0 teams per playoff bracket — respectable volume for a league whose champion is often Miami or a challenger from the middle pack.

Texas Tech is the Big 12's flagship in this model: 33.3% conference title odds, 55.2% playoff frequency, 5.08% national title equity, and 9.88 avg wins. The Red Raiders benefit from a manageable SOS (−3.6) and win the league more often than any peer.

BYU (21.7% playoff) and Utah (19.5%) are the primary challengers. The league carries 10.8% of national title equity — competitive with the ACC — and averages 2.0 teams per field. Oklahoma State (4.83 avg wins) sits at the bottom of a league that rewards consistency over brand.

The American is a wide-open autobid race: Navy leads at 14.9% conference title odds, but South Florida (14.2%, 9.4% playoff) and East Carolina (13.4%) are co-favorites in practice. A league member appears in 32.2% of all playoff simulations.

South Florida averages 8.22 wins — the best record in the league — yet Navy's schedule profile keeps the Midshipmen atop the title odds. The American's path is almost entirely about winning the conference; national title equity across the league totals just 0.19%.

James Madison is the Sun Belt's strongest program by every metric that matters: 32.6% conference title odds, 8.86 avg wins, and 7.1% playoff field rate — higher than many Power Four teams. The Dukes win the league in nearly a third of all simulations.

Old Dominion (17.2% conf title) is the primary challenger. A Sun Belt team appears in 13.9% of playoff fields — almost always JMU. The league's SOS (−11.5 for the Dukes) limits ceiling, but the autobid path is real and the win totals are not fluky.

Boise State leads the Pac-12 footprint at 33.0% conference title odds and 8.4% playoff frequency — the league's only program with meaningful national relevance at 0.13% title equity. The Broncos average 8.27 wins and dominate the conference championship game when they arrive.

Washington State (14.4%) and Texas State (14.9%) chase the autobid. A Pac-12 member appears in 16.5% of playoff fields. This is a survivor's league — programs without a Power Four home fighting for one bid and the legitimacy that comes with it.

Toledo leads the MAC at 24.5% conference title odds with 8.4 avg wins and 4.3% playoff field rate — the league's best autobid case. Miami (OH) (17.4% conf title) and Ohio (13.9%) push the Rockets in a league where one loss can end the season's meaning.

MAC teams face the weakest SOS in FBS (Toledo at −14.9). A league member reaches the playoff field in 9.8% of simulations. The path is narrow — win the conference, accept a low seed, and hope the bracket breaks right.

UNLV tops the Mountain West at 24.9% conference title odds, but North Dakota State is the league's playoff story: 5.5% field rate, 8.25 avg wins, and 20.6% conference title odds. The Bison are the model's strongest G6 bid candidate outside James Madison.

A Mountain West member appears in 17.7% of playoff fields. Air Force (11.9% conf title) and New Mexico (14.2%) add depth to a league where the autobid race is genuinely contested. Total national title equity is 0.08% — autobid or bust.

Conference USA is the thinnest autobid race in FBS: Western Kentucky leads at 18.9% title odds, Liberty follows at 18.3%, and no CUSA team carries measurable national championship equity. A league member reaches the playoff in just 3.7% of simulations.

Liberty (7.44 avg wins, 1.6% playoff) is the most likely bid winner if WKU falters. Jacksonville State (15.9% conf title) adds a third contender. The league's SOS profiles (−9 to −15) make win totals the primary currency — and even 7–5 might not be enough.
